title: CompletableFuture & Reactive
summary: Async programming with CompletableFuture, chaining, combining, error handling, and reactive patterns.

sections.content:
## Why Async?

Blocking threads waiting on I/O wastes CPU resources. In a service with 1000 concurrent requests each waiting 100ms for a DB call, you need 1000 threads with blocking I/O — but only a handful with async.

`CompletableFuture` (Java 8+) provides a **non-blocking, composable** API for async computation.

sections.title: Blocking vs Non-Blocking I/O
sections.content:
graph LR
    subgraph Blocking
        T1[Thread 1] -->|waits| DB1[DB Query]
        T2[Thread 2] -->|waits| DB2[DB Query]
        T3[Thread 3] -->|waits| DB3[DB Query]
    end
    subgraph Non-Blocking
        T4[1 Thread] -->|async| DB4[DB Query]
        T4 -->|async| DB5[DB Query]
        T4 -->|async| DB6[DB Query]
    end
    style T4 fill:#059669,color:#fff

## CompletableFuture Basics

```java
// Start async computation
CompletableFuture<String> future = CompletableFuture.supplyAsync(() -> {
    return fetchUserFromDB(userId); // runs in ForkJoinPool
});

// Chain transformations
CompletableFuture<String> result = future
    .thenApply(user -> user.toUpperCase())      // transform result
    .thenCompose(name -> fetchOrders(name))      // flat-map (async)
    .thenAccept(orders -> System.out.println(orders)) // consume
    .exceptionally(ex -> { log(ex); return null; });  // handle error
```

### Key methods:
| Method | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `supplyAsync(Supplier)` | Start async task with result |
| `runAsync(Runnable)` | Start async task, no result |
| `thenApply(Function)` | Transform result (like map) |
| `thenCompose(Function)` | Chain async steps (like flatMap) |
| `thenCombine(CF, BiFunction)` | Combine two futures |
| `allOf(CFs...)` | Wait for all to complete |
| `anyOf(CFs...)` | Complete when first finishes |

sections.title: CompletableFuture Pipeline
sections.content:
graph LR
    A[supplyAsync\nfetch user] -->|thenApply| B[transform\nuser → name]
    B -->|thenCompose| C[supplyAsync\nfetch orders]
    C -->|thenCombine| D[combine with\nfetch inventory]
    D -->|thenAccept| E[process result]
    A -->|exceptionally| F[handle error]
    C -->|exceptionally| F
    style A fill:#4f46e5,color:#fff
    style E fill:#059669,color:#fff
    style F fill:#dc2626,color:#fff

sections.title: Don't call .get() or .join() in reactive chains
sections.content: Calling .get() or .join() blocks the calling thread, defeating the purpose of async. Only call them at the very end of a pipeline, or use thenAccept/thenRun to consume results asynchronously.

sections.question: What is the difference between thenApply() and thenCompose()?
options: thenApply runs synchronously, thenCompose runs asynchronously
thenApply transforms a result (like map), thenCompose chains another async future (like flatMap)
thenCompose is for error handling, thenApply is for success
They are identical, just different naming conventions
sections.explanation: thenApply() is like map — it transforms a value T to U synchronously. thenCompose() is like flatMap — it takes a value T and returns a new CompletableFuture<U>, enabling chaining of async operations without nesting.
